Welcome back everybody. Now we're getting toward the finish. We're going to talk about what to actually write in the letter itself. You've got your headline, right? And that sucked them in, that got their attention. And so then you have, if you're doing kind of a letter, dear, dear whoever. And then you have the lead, right? We call that the lead. So what do you say there? How do you open? The first mistake a lot of people make is that they start out with something that doesn't connect from the headline, right? So if you say something in the headline, if you say how to make a million dollars, you want right away to say in your copy, hey, you're interested in making a million dollars. Let me tell you more about that blah, blah, blah. Here's a story of someone that made a million dollars. If you say civil war is coming in Germany, start out with that. You'd be surprised how many promotions you see where there's something interesting in the headline. It brings in the reader, but then they don't follow up here. So that's really the most important thing is to keep that connection. You've got them to start reading the letter because of something you said in the headline. Don't disconnect from that. So a couple of things that you can do when you open, because it's almost as important as the headline. It's a good place to lose people. And you don't want to lose people. You want to keep them reading. So one thing you can do is you can have some sort of a shocking fact. If you do how to make a million dollars, and it's not marked it, did you know that there are 1,000 millionaires being made every day in Germany? 1,000 new millionaires? Whoa, that kind of now kind of keeps me reading, keeps me going. For arthritis, you promised them a cure for arthritis. Did you know there are 10,000 people suffer from arthritis? Did you know that there's a new breakthrough for curing arthritis? Some kind of a fact, something they didn't know before. Something new. The best way to get someone to stop reading your letter is to tell them something they already know. You tell them, oh, did you know arthritis is a very painful disease? Oh, thanks. I knew that. Go away. So always be new. Always be different. Always be unique. People want to know what's different. What's unique? What can you tell me I don't know already? Hurry up. Tell me something good. The other thing you can do is you can start to tell some kind of a story. You know, how to, um, purchasing agents, how you can never, never get blindsided by the rising price of raw materials again, right? So you could start with a shocking fact about how the price of raw materials goes up and down so fast. Or you could start with a story. You could say, um, hey, let me tell you about a purchasing manager who purchased, you know, 180 tons of copper and she purchased it for a dollar a ton. And then she woke up the next morning and the price of copper, there was a big, they found a new supply of copper. So the price of copper dropped to 80 cents instead of a dollar. And her boss was so angry and blah, blah, blah. Don't let this ever happen to you. It's a little bit of a nightmare story. So that's, that's a really nice way to open, to open a letter. Sometimes you can open with empathy. How to make a million dollars in the stock market. You know, um, you know, I know what it's like to be an investor and to watch your money disappear day after day, right? How to cure arthritis. I know what it's like to have arthritis. I had arthritis 20 years ago when I, when I first became a doctor and I tried all the medical stuff and it didn't work. So finally I went on my own and I researched herbs, you know, so I'm telling how much I empathize with you. I understand. I used to have arthritis. I used to be a purchasing agent. Now I'm writing a newsletter. I used to, I used to be an investor that didn't know how to invest money properly. I feel your pain. I understand your pain. I know what it's like to be you. I'm a lot like you. If you can make that connection in the beginning of a letter, it's very powerful and it'll make people keep reading because they feel like you're writing to them, right? And you always want them to have that feeling. It's like not that you're talking to a big group of people, you know, ladies and gentlemen, when you invest in, it has to be like, Hey, I'm talking to you. Look, I know it's painful to be an investor these days. You don't know what crazy thing Donald Trump is going to do. You don't know what, you know, who's the next person, who's the next country to leave the European Union? How is that going to affect your money? It's so crazy today. I know what that's like and I want to help you because I care about you. So show that. You know, you always, just like in the headline in books, but especially here, you want to feel them to feel it's about them. You, talking to you. I know what you go through. I know what your problems are. And the other thing is promise plus proof. If you haven't made a big promise in your headline, then this is the place to do it because people need that promise. They need that benefit. They need to know what's in it for me. So if you haven't strongly answered that in the headline, you want to answer it in the lead. And if you haven't proven what you've said in the headline, right? If you haven't said, you know, Harvard study says there's a way to cure arthritis. Warren Buffett says this is the best method for investing I've ever seen. This is the method I use. If you don't have that in your headline, have it here, right? How to make a million dollars. You probably don't really believe you can make a million dollars. But let me tell you about these 10 people that use this method to make a million dollars. Let me show you that this is the same method Warren Buffett used to make a million dollars. Proof right away, right in the lead. There's proof of what you've said. One of the other things too, and this is, I guess, when I say shocking fact, a good thing to do in the lead is to have something that's like a myth or contradicts, like a big lie, something that they believe to be true that isn't true. If you can work that, I'm going to show you how to make a million dollars. I'm going to show you how to cure arthritis. But I'm also going to show you about the big lie that you're being told. I'm also going to show you something that 99% of investors believe, something that the media keeps saying that is untrue and is what's keeping you from getting rich. So now I not only want to know about how to make a million dollars, but I also want to know what that thing is, that big idea, that myth, that big lie that's keeping me from getting rich. Something I want to know what that thing is that I believe that's not true and that other people believe isn't true. That's so powerful and you'll see that a lot in really good copy that it'll include that idea of some kind of myth, something contradicting, something that goes against what do you call it, conventional wisdom. It's like everybody believes this, but I'm going to show you why you shouldn't believe it, why it's keeping you unhealthy. It's keeping you not being a good purchasing manager. It's keeping you from being the best investor you can be. So we've got the lead, we've bonded with them, we've shown some proof, we've shown them why it's interesting, they should keep reading, maybe we've told them the story, we empathize with them. After the lead, what do you do? Well we talked about how you could use bullets. You can use bullets to say you have your lead here and then you could say, for example, bullet, bullet, bullet, and then maybe you take a bullet and you make it into text. Like, oh there's a big mistake many people make. There's three things that are important. There's whatever it is, you expand that into text. So that's one way. The other way you want to keep, you want to tell them about the solution, why the solution is important. A lot of the body text of your letter should be why the way we do what we do is a better way, why it's better to have four investors, why it's better to invest using our technique, why it's better to use natural methods. You want to be proving that. You want to show, look, look what drugs do to you. Here's why you shouldn't do drugs. Look what this study says. This study says drugs kill people. This study says drugs don't really cure arthritis. It's really just sort of a band aid that masks the pain. More proof, more reasons why the way we do things is the better way to do things. And then eventually you bring in the product at some point. So there's kind of a decision that you have to make when you write copy. Do I talk about the product in the beginning or do I wait to bring in the product? Some people say that's indirect versus direct. Direct is, hey, I got a great product for you. It's a newsletter. Here it is. Let me tell you about it. Indirect is more, oh, you have these problems. Here's some great ways to do things. The way to do it is to have more information. You should invest in this type of thing. And then finally, now let me show you my product that will make it easy for you and quick for you to do these things. So it's kind of a sequence of you do it. And I think we might have talked about this when we talked about problem solution. You talk about the problem. You talk about a general solution, natural medicine, a certain way of investing, value investing. Maybe the general solution is to be more aware of what laws are being passed about human resources. Oh, but this is very hard to do. Where do you get this information? How do you get good information about value investing? How do you, like Sonja, your solution is investing in different parts of the world, right? Your solution is that investors should not just concentrate on where they live or shouldn't just invest in the United States. They should be able to invest in all the regions. So maybe you talk about that for a while before you even mention that you have a newsletter. And then you say, hey, you know what the easiest way to do that is? It's to have your own expert in each of these places. And so now the product comes. We're the hero. We help you do it quickly, easily. You don't have to do all this work yourself. You don't have to read every law about human resources that the legislature passes, right? You don't have to study every stock and figure out whether it's a good investment or not. We do it for you. A lot of what I think a lot of products are and a lot of, especially what newsletters are, it's like we do this for you. We do it for you so you don't have to do all the work. We save you the time. So you want to talk a little bit about the product, why it's so good. We're good at doing that, right? We know how to do that. We know how to talk about our product. Who it's for, who not for. This is really important, I think, in the copy, is to have some discussion of this is the type of person that this is for. And a lot of times you want to make this be the type of person is the role that they want to step into, right? This is for people that want to be able to fix their own computers, right? These are for people who like tinkering with things. This newsletter is for people that like to be the people that other people come to for advice. That's who this newsletter is for. Who's this newsletter not for? It's not for people that aren't willing to learn new things. It's not for people who want to call someone to repair their computer every time something goes wrong. It's not for people who are afraid to take a screwdriver and look inside their computer and see what's going on. And so the kind of person kind of goes, no, I don't want to be that kind of person. I don't want to be the person that's helpless, right? I want to be the person that's the hero. I guess I need this. I guess I need to buy this. So think about that. Think about who your product is for. Think about who it's not for. Put it into the copy. Overcoming objections. And I think we talked about how thinking about objections is a great way to generate copy that you think, oh, what would they say about this? Oh, it's too expensive. Okay. So now I write some copy about why it's really not that expensive. Oh, it's hard work. A lot of people think the things we talk about for investing or for health sometimes. Oh, I don't want to do all that work. So we write about how easy it is, right? How simple, how we make it easy. Again, these are the kind of things that you want to be sure to put into your body copy. One important thing, I think, well, there's two traps I think people get into. One is they start to explain too much. They start to explain, okay, this is how we do it. This is why this is important. And they get lost in just explaining. So you always want to envision like you're talking to someone. You always want to be able to see their eyes glazing over. You want to see them falling asleep. And when you see that happening, you know, okay, I got to wake them up. Hey, you know, Zimone, this is really important because of this. You know, you can make a lot of, okay, he's awake again. And how it works is blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. And it works by blah, blah, blah. Oops, he's falling asleep. Too much talking about that. Got to talk about something that gets Zimone interested again. So it's like when you're writing copy, it's like you're just writing at your desk. But you've got to be thinking about it like you're having a conversation with a reader. So you write something and you say, you know, you can make a million dollars with this. And then you see the reader going, yeah, right, I can make a right. So yeah, yes, you will. I know it sounds, it doesn't sound true, but it really is. And here's why it's true, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. And you want to hear the reader asking questions. How does this work? Why is this different? You want to hear how the reader reacts to things, just like you would react to things. And then when you write the copy, you're answering those questions. This is different because blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. It works by blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. But again, don't get too lost in how it works because you lose people. The last thing I really want to say about copy is one mistake people make a lot, I think, is that they tend to write copy like, here are some benefits of what my thing does. Here's some proof that it really does it. Here's an explanation of how it works. And so you get like, this is the benefits. This is the proof. This is the explanation of how it works. And the best copy is where all those things go on at the same time. And they kind of, I can't do with my hands. But they interweave with each other. They're all together happening at the same time. The best example to me is something like, let's say you're writing about weight loss. And you say, this incredible new type of exercise will enable you to lose three dress sizes in a week. And you'll be astonished by that just as thousands of women have been already that have been successfully using this product. So you see how in the same sentence I've said benefit, you lose three dress sizes in a week. I've put in how it works. It works through exercise. Maybe I said a little bit about burning fat. I would include that in there. This incredible exercise helps you lose three dress sizes in two weeks by raising your metabolism so you burn more fat. And that may sound incredible to you just as it sounded incredible to thousands of women who have already used it to lose weight. So I've woven in the proof. I've woven in the proof, the benefit, the explanation, all in like one paragraph. That's what you want to aim for when you're writing copy. You don't want to be like, oh, I got to do all the benefit. I got a list. I hate when people do lists of things. They do like these lists. Oh, here's a benefit. It does this. Benefit. Does this. Does this. Does this. I think people's eyes glaze over when you do that. Because you'd never be sitting in a bar and talking to someone and say, hey, I got this great new product. It relieves back pain. It lowers your stress. You wouldn't know. You'd say it's a great product. I tried it. When I tried it, I was so stressed out when I first tried it. And then within a week, I didn't have any stress. And what was really good too was I lost weight because of it. We don't talk in lists like that. So be really careful about lists. Be really skeptical when you see yourself making a list. Turn it into a conversation with the person. Use it. Do it the way you would actually be talking to someone if you were describing it to them.
